dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a cardiac arrest/death call at a [CITY] address.
Hi, I'm Alex, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you clarify the events that led to this arrest?
The patient was found on the floor in cardiac arrest. No one witnessed the arrest event.
The patient is unresponsive, with no eye opening, verbal response, or motor response. GCS is 3.
The patient is pulseless with blood pressure of 0/0, respirations of 12, SpO2 of 78%, and ekg shows PEA.
We have the patient's demographic information; this is a 60-year-old female.
The patient's medications include bisacodyl, amiodarone, clopidogrel, insulin lispro, albuterol, acetaminophen, gabapentin, and heparin.
No known medication allergies for this patient.
Cleaning and clearing the oropharynx with suction as part of airway management.
Placing oropharyngeal airway to ensure airway patency and assist with breathing.
Assisting ventilations using BVM with mask at 15 liters of oxygen.
Initiating manual CPR after identifying no spontaneous circulation and no respirations.
Applying 4-lead ECG monitoring. The rhythm remains PEA.
Accessing the left humeral intraosseous for medication administration.
Establishing a 20-gauge IV in the right wrist for fluid and drug delivery.
Switching to the AutoPulse device for mechanical CPR.
First intubation with a McGrath scope was attempted but unsuccessful.
Administering 1mg of epinephrine via intraosseous access per protocol.
Second intubation successful with a 7.5 ET tube confirmed at 24 cm from the lips.
The airway is secure with verified tube placement and return of capnography waveform.
Blood glucose level on arrival is elevated at 177 mg/dL.
Administering 1 mEq of sodium bicarbonate per protocol due to wide QRS and capnography.
Skin condition reported as warm and dry with no signs of trauma recorded in the notes.
No head or neck trauma observed. Pupils could not be assessed due to tube intubation.
Oropharynx and airway are clear with no obstruction or secretions.
Bilateral rales present but equal with assisted breathing via the ET tube.
Abdomen is soft with a hernia-type mass noted during the secondary survey.
Extremities show full range of motion with good peripheral motor sensation.
The patient has no reported pain on examination, as per vitals and clinical review.
Known history includes non-insulin-dependent diabetes, congestive heart failure, and end-stage renal disease.
Additional notes: hypothyroidism, peripheral vascular disease, and hyperlipidemia.
Continuing compressions with AutoPulse and ventilations supported by the ET tube.
Recent rhythm check shows persistently PEA with no signs of return of spontaneous circulation.
Vital signs remain unchanged: PEA rhythm, no pulse, O2 saturation of 78%, and respiratory rate 12.
Continuing cardiac arrest protocol with repeated epinephrine doses and assisted ventilation.
Using a controlled 6-man lift to secure the patient on the stretcher for safe transport.
Transporting the patient to [COMPANY_NAME] for advanced care.
Full patient report given to the receiving hospital staff without incident.
All interventions - including Advanced Life Support performed with protocol adherence.
Call time recorded continuously from 09:06 AM as per ePCR documentation.
Not evaluated due to ongoing acute arrest and time-sensitive transportation.
Airway remains maintained with repeating breath sounds bilateral and equal.
Assisted ventilations ongoing via BVM connected to the ET tube.
Second 1mg epinephrine dose given at 09:19 via IO access per arrest protocol.
IV line intact, no swelling or infiltration noted, taped and running free of kinks.
Dosing continues: epinephrine next at 09:36, all per documentation and protocol.
Lungs auscultated equal bilaterally throughout transport via BVM assisted technique.
GCS is still 3 as the patient remained unresponsive per observation.
No external trauma found upon transport and move, as reported and assessed by crew.
Existing drug history and mg regimen documented in the electronic patient care report.
No documented temperature anomaly, normal skin temperature as assessed.
SpO2 remains at 78% despite oxygen support at 15LPM.
All drug use and IV/ORP interventions logged per time-stamped entries as directed.
Patient secured in optimal recumbent position per fall arrest and sudden transport.
Directing patient to [COMPANY_NAME] for full resuscitation care as per protocol.
All clinical documentation double-checked for accuracy at time of transport.
Full team notes filled out for post-call transfer and regulatory review.
All RFD and medical crew signatures completed and noted per dispatch record.
Incident location noted and GPS history captured in dispatch logs without intervention.
All steps taken in line with cardiac arrest guideline S2-7 and local protocol.
Hernia mass documented and recorded as soft and non-pulsating in secondary assessment.
Full handover given and confirmed with receiving ER staff on patient file transfer.
All signs show patient unable to sign due to being in arrest without consciousness.
PEA rhythm with no return of organized rhythm on real-time review with hospital.
All transport field summaries and incident notations submitted as part of medical logs.
Whole care plan fully designed for patient non-cooperation as per ongoing arrest plateau.
Sodium bicarbonate given at 09:30 due to prolonged arrest and wide QRS noted on EKG.
All on-call care facilities and clinical interventions delivered as per protocol timing.
General equipment change-out routine complete and flagged for next EMS call.
Communications with hospital taken periodically to ensure seamless transition of care.
All statuses and actions marked as transport-stage "hot" as documented by ePCR reports.
Patient kept warm, dry, and secured per general emergency resuscitation process.
CPR procedures finalized upon patient handover and documentation transfer completed.
All operational checkboxes completed as part of post-transport standard procedure.
Crew debrief completed on site for hospital requirements under this care plan.
Call marked complete with all dispatched resources and support now available.
No future actions planned for the case at this time.
EPCR paperwork and digitized logs submitted for hospital and CRM processing.
All forms approved and submitted to EMS CRC for regulatory and legal tracking.
Unit returned to active service at 09:47 with no remaining patient tasks required.
Call-log resources and dispatch markers removed with normal operational priority.
Equipment restocked and ready for next emergency call pre-determined.
Electronic Patient Care Report uploaded full and complete for institutional opt-ins.
Crew relieved of shift at station after hospital transfer and full procedural compliance.
No changes required in call logs for further action based on current documentation.
Crew at standby for next call as of 09:48 with no ongoing patient cases.
All medical charting updated in CRM and hospital information system with time references.
Patient remains non-responsive per clinical assessments throughout transport.
No discrepancies found between clinical notes and official EPCR record.
Call software action noted in internal logs as final data for audit.
Vehicle and all gear cleaned per post-scene safety protocols.
All team members provided with wellness support post-event as protocol recommends.
Transport activities terminated and no further patient contact ongoing.
All patient forms and response reports closed and filed.
